findings: Iteration 1: the BC interpreter already had every major execution-speed optimization implemented from prior work (threaded/computed-goto dispatch, per-invocation register-file pooling, fused compare+branch superinstructions, constant-operand immediate folding, a linear register allocator) but none were the default. Flipping BCInterpreterBackend.cpp's four option defaults to the fastest already-validated combination cut the geomean of exec_bc_add/fibonacci/sum from 82455ns to 57424ns (-30.4%) with zero new code paths.

Iteration 2: BCLoweringProvider::process(BasicBlockInvocation) -- which lowers block-invocation arguments, i.e. loop-carried values like fibonacci's a/b/i on a back-edge -- unconditionally routed every already-bound argument through a fresh temp register (2 REG_MOVs each), even for simple non-cyclic chains. Replaced it with the classic parallel-copy sequentialization used for SSA-destruction/phi lowering: emit a direct MOV wherever safe, fall back to exactly one temp per genuine permutation cycle (e.g. a literal swap). This dropped the geomean further, from 57424ns to 38094ns (-33.7%), a cumulative -53.8% from the original baseline; fibonacci's time nearly halved on its own (277884ns -> ~112000ns).

Iteration 2 validation was the bulk of the work: the differential fuzzer surfaced additional findings at 300k-trial scale, but investigation showed the fuzzer's pointer/memory-domain test cases have a non-deterministic native-oracle 'expected' value even for byte-identical scalar arguments (confirmed by re-running the same survey size repeatedly on the same unchanged binary) -- i.e. those cases depend on runtime heap addresses/uninitialized memory, a pre-existing harness artifact, not a logic bug. This was confirmed by running the identical trial counts against the pre-change commit in a throwaway worktree (same finding categories, same address-dependent non-determinism) and by writing a new, deterministic, targeted regression test (BCRegisterCoalescingTest.cpp) that specifically forces 2- and 3-element register permutation cycles plus a cycle mixed with an independent chained value, checked against the independent, non-compiling 'interpreter' execution mode. All passed; the full BC execution-test suite also grew from 32/32 (6987 assertions) to 33/33 (7050 assertions) and stayed green.

Iteration 3 (discarded): implemented a general induction-variable strength-reduction IR pass (StrengthReductionPass, registered in the shared LegacyCompiler pipeline so it would benefit every backend) that replaces array[i]'s per-iteration base+i*stride recomputation with a single pointer incremented by stride each iteration. This required adding new IR graph-mutation primitives that had no prior precedent in the codebase (no pass had ever inserted new operations or new loop-carried arguments before) and uncovered a real, non-obvious gotcha: control-flow edges rebind every argument to a *fresh* per-block alias object even on simple pass-through edges, so induction-variable and loop-invariance detection both had to resolve full alias-equivalence classes via BFS, not raw pointer identity. After two dominance/insertion bugs found via crashes, the transform became correct: validated by the full execution-test suite (33/33, 7050 assertions), a new deterministic regression test (2-/3-cycle permutations checked against the independent 'interpreter' execution mode), and roughly 1M differential-fuzzer trials (with careful investigation ruling out the fuzzer's pointer-domain non-determinism as a false lead). Despite being correct, it regressed the target benchmark by ~9-17% (sum: 23.7-23.9M ns -> 27.9M ns) because the BC interpreter's cost is dispatch-dominated, not ALU-dominated: eliminating one multiply while adding a new stride-add, a dead cast that was never cleaned up, an unfolded 'ptr+0', and one extra loop-carried register (extra REG_MOVs from iteration 2's coalescing) is a net loss. Discarded per the keep/discard protocol; all code reverted, working tree clean.

Follow-up note for a future attempt at this same optimization: it would need to (a) also neutralize the now-dead cast operation, not just the multiply, (b) fold the residual 'ptr+0' away (either via a proper dead/redundant-code pass, or by touching LoadOperation/StoreOperation's address operand directly, which this iteration deliberately avoided to limit new API surface), and (c) probably re-measure on an ALU-bound backend (MLIR) rather than the dispatch-bound BC interpreter, where the same transform might genuinely pay off -- a throwaway MLIR-enabled build was set up during this session (build-mlir-check/) but never used for that comparison since the BC-side regression already ruled the experiment out.

config.goal: improve execution performance of the nautilus bytecode interpreter, using the nautilus benchmarks as reference
config.metric: geomean(ns) of exec_bc_add, exec_bc_fibonacci, exec_bc_sum from nautilus-benchmarks
